data.title: Why I Like ULTY But Not Over 50% Yielding YMAX
data.description:
High-yield investors today have numerous ETFs to choose from, and two very popular ones are YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX) and YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY). I had recently written about ULTY with a Buy rating, which may come as a surprise, considering I had previously rated YMAX as a Sell. What may come as an even greater surprise is that I continue to maintain both ratings despite YMAX's outperformance and lower NAV erosion compared to ULTY since the latter's inception. The reason lies behind the fundamental strategies of each ETF. Some value can be found in ULTY's strategy, while YMAX's strategy is questionable and offers minimal value to any portfolio. Income investors should avoid yield chasing, and that starts with looking at the fundamental strategies behind the large distributions.

YMAX's Outperformance Over ULTY

Since ULTY's inception on February 28, 2024, YMAX has outperformed ULTY on both a total return and price return basis. YMAX is down a little over 40%, while ULTY is down over 70%. Of course, we cannot only look at price returns on high-yield funds such as YMAX and ULTY. When dividends are factored in, YMAX has offered total returns of about 11% more than ULTY.

Both funds have become weekly dividend payers as well, with YMAX beginning weekly distributions in September 2024 and ULTY in March 2025. The funds' most recent distributions annualize to approximately 51.72% from YMAX and 84.77% from ULTY, according to YieldMax's website. Furthermore, YMAX has also performed better with less volatility and drawdown in a hypothetical backtest comparison of investing a $10K lump sum in both assets from March 2024 to August 2025 with dividend reinvestment. ULTY's standard deviation was at 23.78% compared to YMAX's 17.77%, and its maximum drawdown also exceeded the latter at -17.77% versus 12.52%.

The Factors Driving YMAX's Performance

With YMAX's seemingly objective outperformance over ULTY so far, one may conclude that YMAX is the better fund. Before investing in anything, however, it is imperative to evaluate what's driving an investment's returns. YMAX is a fund of funds that holds all of YieldMax's single-ticker covered call ETFs in equal weight and rebalances its portfolio monthly. A sample of YMAX's current holdings is displayed below, and the full list can be found here.

For those unfamiliar with YieldMax's single-ticker covered call ETFs, these are funds that run synthetic covered call strategies where the fund managers start by simultaneously selling an at-the-money ("ATM") put option and buying an ATM call option on one asset. This creates a position that emulates a long position in the actual asset (hence the term "synthetic") and allows the fund managers to then sell covered calls. Nowadays, the fund managers will sell credit spreads as well. This is a strategy where YieldMax will buy call options further out-of-the-money ("OTM") to allow for more upside, and it has improved performance during bull markets. Due to the high implied volatility ("IV") levels in many of these single tickers, the funds will generate large monthly distributions that yield in the double-digit and sometimes even triple-digit range.

Naturally, because YMAX holds all of YieldMax's single-ticker covered call funds, it will inherit many of the same characteristics. YMAX's distributions are more or less an average of all the underlying YieldMax single-ticker covered call ETF yields. By the same logic, YMAX's performance will also correlate to the general average performance of YieldMax's single-ticker covered call ETFs. The following two charts are the total and price return comparisons of all the underlying YieldMax single-ticker covered call ETFs that date back to at least January 2024, when YMAX launched.

Given how only two funds of the 18 listed above (AIYY, MRNY) suffered major losses, and another three assets (AMDY, XOMO, XYZY) essentially broke even, it should be no surprise that YMAX generated positive total returns of more than 40% since its inception. 2024 and 2025 have been phenomenal years for many of the assets within YMAX, with notable names such as NFLY and NVDY generating triple-digit returns and many others offering high double-digit returns. Though despite these positive total returns, it is worth pointing out how all of the underlying covered call funds have had negative price returns, which in turn, also leads to NAV erosion in YMAX.

YMAX - Diversification In Disguise

The problem with YMAX is that we have been in a bull market that has been beneficial to nearly all the underlying assets in YMAX. YieldMax launches single-ticker covered call funds to be included in YMAX without a clear theme, except perhaps popularity. Here's what YMAX's sector mix currently looks like:

While some investors may look at this and describe YMAX as a diversified high-yield fund, that is not quite the case. YMAX only has 34 holdings at the time of writing, excluding its cash and cash-like positions. Granted, its OARK and GDXY holdings are funds that sell synthetic covered calls on the ARKK and GDX ETFs, which each have 46 and 51 holdings, respectively. Still, the fund offers very limited diversification compared to indices such as the S&P 500 or the NASDAQ 100, and more importantly, a theme. The S&P 500, after all, is an index of U.S. large-cap stocks. YMAX has included both small caps and large caps, U.S. and Chinese stocks, and alternative assets such as Bitcoin alongside equities. As previously mentioned, there's no particular theme or objective behind YMAX's holdings other than the equal weighting of all of YieldMax's single-ticker covered call ETFs. The fund will simply include any single-ticker covered call ETF that YieldMax launches, and those tickers can represent anything with a sizable options market.

Selling covered calls is also known as a buy-write strategy. The first part of a buy-write is buy, which means you have to want to like the underlying asset, or more specifically, in YMAX's case, the long synthetic exposure to those stocks. The case where you want equal weight exposure to the particular underlying assets in YMAX's holdings with a covered call or credit spread overlay is pretty much non-existent. Not all underlying assets within YMAX's single-ticker covered call funds are suitable for covered call strategies all the time, and certainly not in an equal-weight format where positive returns from assets like NVDY and NFLY are actively being reallocated to assets with negative returns, such as AIYY and MRNY. You also have to keep in mind that YMAX charges an additional management expense of 0.29% on top of the 0.99% fees and expenses from the underlying holdings. You could argue the extra fees are justified due to YMAX's weekly distributions and active tax-loss harvesting strategies that potentially make distributions more tax-efficient, but the opportunity cost is far greater considering the alternatives in today's market.

The Premise of Buying ULTY

One alternative to buying YMAX is YieldMax's very own ULTY. Now, returns since ULTY's inception and its management fees certainly have not looked better than YMAX. If we refer back to the first chart in this article, you'll see that ULTY is down over 70% since its inception and has offered weaker total returns than YMAX. It does not help ULTY either that its net expense ratio is 1.30% and actually 0.02% higher than YMAX's. Thus, with higher fees and underperformance, why would anyone invest in ULTY over YMAX?

ULTY is primarily saved by a strategy adjustment made earlier around March, where the fund opted to use option collar strategies that protect the fund's downside. This is where the fund managers would buy protective puts alongside selling calls, and I explained the strategy more in my previous article on ULTY. The result is that since March 2025, ULTY has shown generally similar performances to YMAX with a small outperformance and larger payouts.

As you can see from the charts above, ULTY does not escape YMAX's NAV erosion issues and, in fact, has it worse. But when it comes to investing in high-yield funds, reinvestment is a must, and evaluation of the fund's future ability to generate total returns and distributions is more important. The difference-maker between ULTY and YMAX lies in the assets that YieldMax chooses to sell options on. YMAX holds all of YieldMax's single-ticker covered call ETFs in equal weight. ULTY, on the other hand, will frequently adjust its holdings to maximize income, and doing so is at the complete discretion of the fund managers. From ULTY's prospectus:

The Fund's allocation to particular Underlying Securities is primarily driven by implied volatility levels. The Adviser strategically identifies Underlying Securities in periods of likely higher volatility (e.g., ahead of significant events, like earnings releases). The Adviser will typically select between fifteen and thirty Underlying Securities and will implement the Options Strategies on the Underlying Securities. However, when the Adviser deems it appropriate, it may choose as few as five Underlying Securities on which to implement the Options Strategies."

We can already see this in action where ULTY's holdings and trades in BULL and APP from a little more than a week ago, when I last covered the fund, are no longer there. Now, we'll see BLSH and BMNR among the first three names.

This makes buying ULTY akin to paying a professional trader to trade your money for you, and it comes with all of the same risks. In bull markets that carry all assets up, it will often be easier for traders to turn profits, while in down markets, it'll be much more difficult. Either way, however, the mindset of a professional trader is to consistently take profits over the long run while minimizing losses. To do this, traders will set up risk-to-reward frameworks, and it is something that has become evident in ULTY. Since the change to weekly distributions, ULTY's distributions have had a standard deviation of 0.74%. YMAX, on the other hand, has been at 4.39%.

Why ULTY Over YMAX

For high-yield investors looking to enhance their income, ULTY is more desirable than YMAX because it can consistently maintain high yields regardless of the market. By trading in high-IV assets, having ULTY in your portfolio is like paying YieldMax to trade some of the hottest stocks. Of course, traders are not guaranteed to make money, especially on the highly volatile assets that ULTY trades on, which is why allocations should be limited. Like I previously mentioned in my last article on ULTY, a use case for ULTY may be to have a small allocation in combination with more stable assets like dividend growth ETFs and stocks to create a desired blended yield for your personal income situation. With YMAX, however, you are simply holding all of YieldMax's single-ticker covered call ETFs in equal weight, and I see little reason why anyone would want to do that. There are opportunity costs for every investment decision we make, and the opportunity cost for YMAX without considering any other providers outside of YieldMax is holding individual YieldMax single-ticker funds that you have a strong conviction on, the thematic option income funds, or something like ULTY. All of these alternatives can be supported by investment theses that can help your decision-making. Hence, I maintain both my Sell and Buy ratings on YMAX and ULTY, respectively.

data.title: Aritzia: Buy Rated Into Earnings With Headwinds Abating
data.description:
Aritzia (TSX:ATZ:CA) has been one of the better-performing discretionary stocks over the past number of months. I wrote about ATZ more than a year ago in May 2024 when the shares were significantly undervalued due to growing pains. Now the company is on a much stronger footing, giving long-term investors confidence that they can continue to make significant share gains in the years ahead. That said, we all saw the volatility of Trump's tariff policy rock the world in April, with tariffs on both Vietnam and China. Aritzia continues to have a strong position regardless, with diversification through North America and with production located in many different jurisdictions around the world. However, the Aritzia brand has continued to show strong momentum in the United States amid its high-value proposition for an affordable price. This can continue into future years with very low penetration of both the brand and stores across the country. The focus on all areas of fashion shelters them from issues some other retailers may be having that focus too much on one category, like athleisure. While the Canadian market is likely to grow at a moderate pace, the American operations continue to be one of the best growth stories in retail going forward. As the strength of the business model keeps taking shape, I continue to rate ATZ a buy going into the October 9th fiscal Q2 earnings.

Fiscal Q1—Customer Base Spreading in the United States

The story of Aritzia continues to be one of exceptional strength from the US side of the business. ATZ enjoyed a 40% year-over-year increase in US guests as well as a 45% increase in revenues, up to $413 million in Q2. The brand is resonating as ATZ continues to add boutiques in major shopping areas at a steady but not spectacular pace. The Covid era was good to Aritzia because the company significantly improved its website and online presence, now paying dividends. Digital sales have remained a high portion of the business, which is essential since many states still don't have an Aritzia location but may have knowledge of the brand. This is with a modest low single-digit portion of sales going to digital marketing, with big gains in engagement for a very reasonable cost. Management continues to hit the right notes to grow the business at a good pace and rebound from issues in 2024. Inventory was up a modest 3% y/y, showing continued discipline from management. This leads to reduced markdowns and efficient supply chains - essential to retail businesses in the current environment. Adjusted earnings were up 97.4%, showing how well the company has performed over the past year and brought down margin cost pressures.

Even through the lens of tariffs that are ongoing from countries such as Vietnam and China, the company is going to continue to improve gross margins. This is at odds with many in the industry who are struggling to keep margins level in the current regime. Cambodia at 20% and Vietnam at 20%, as expected, currently have a negative 50 basis points of margin pressure. China is currently at 30%, pending the trade deal still in negotiations, a level acceptable considering the costs of production in the country. While the exact breakdown between countries isn't on SEC filings, it will be a modest hit to the margin line in 2025 as we go forward. That said, most retail supply chains have already diversified production in South Asia, making the overall tariff rate of China less important than it would have been 10 years ago. In the end, the moderate tariff scenarios we have seen since April will still allow ATZ to nicely improve margins into year-end with gains to be made in 2026 as well with scale benefits.

Geographic expansion is essential to improve brand recognition in the huge United States market. This fiscal year the company is adding a measured 11 new US boutiques and one in Canada. The outperformance of US boutiques and benefits for the e-commerce business have led to most investment being south of the border for the foreseeable future. Aritzia is eyeing a total of 150 US locations, more than double the current number, which should have significant benefits in brand awareness and growth. The company is adding these in the best jurisdictions first, with a measured approach showing management's ability to focus on operating leverage and profitability while growing. This has helped the company continue to purchase shares under its buyback plan - up to 4.2 million until May 6, 2026, if any pullback occurs. For now, look for ATZ to continue to build its cash position, which could be helpful if any acquisition opportunities arise in a very difficult retail market.

Revenue growth estimates have been increasing over time and have been a large reason for the outperformance so far in 2025. This is due to strong performance in the United States and a resilient performance in the stores in Canada, which are much more mature in age. The shares are now trading at 32x forward earnings, which is in the high end of the range for a company in the retail space. Share price appreciation from here will be more supported by continued improvements in margins and scale rather than from the trading multiple, which is likely to stay flat or decline into year-end 2026. Gross margin increased 320 basis points over the past year heading into fiscal Q2, and more increases are possible even as tariffs continue to slow down the pace of margin expansion.

The stock is in a trading range currently around $85, although it has been able to eke out some new highs recently. Recent weakness in the overall apparel space in names like Lululemon and Nike may be weighing on sentiment. Young women are getting squeezed by inflation through food and rent, with apparel one of the easiest areas to cut spending. That makes the strong revenue performance of Aritzia even more impressive when considering the current macro.

Aritzia—Buy Rated Into Earnings

Aritzia has continued to be one of the only retail companies showing underlying strong growth and market share gains. The company continues to add square footage in the United States and make inroads with its value proposition. As the footprint becomes more US-centric, growth should be able to continue to flourish, leading to solid equity gains over time, as we have seen in the recent years. ATZ continues to manage through difficult tariff and margin pressures in recent years. This helped the management team reach a seasoned level regarding the business, improving my confidence in the medium-term vision of 2-5 years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch
data.description:
It has appeared as if the Federal Reserve has slowed down the speed at which it is reducing the size of its securities portfolio.

Over the past five weeks, the amount of securities held outright by the Fed has dropped by $4.7 billion, or less than $1.0 billion per week.

This is certainly less than the amount the Fed was shrinking its securities portfolio by over the previous year, going back to the middle of September in 2024.

From the middle of September 2024 to the last week of August 2025, the amount of securities held outright by the Federal Reserve dropped by right around $360.0 billion—an average of around $30.0 billion per month.

Although the Federal Reserve has not announced that it was working on another "round" of quantitative tightening—or some other "change of pace" connected with the "quantitative" management of the securities portfolio, this significant drop-off in the size of the reductions lends some credibility to such a move.

We need to keep an eye out for such a possibility—especially coming right at the time that the Fed reduced its policy rate of interest—the target range for the Federal Funds rate.

Now, with the market seemingly expecting at least one more reduction—if not two in the rest of the year—it might be expected that the Fed would modify the rate at which it was managing the securities portfolio.

Going forward, however, this is something we need to watch very closely.

One thing we can watch is the reserve balances held by the commercial banks, the "excess reserves" that the Fed maintains.

On September 24, 2025, the amount of excess reserves in the banking system totalled $2,999.7 billion. This was only the second time that the level of excess reserves in the commercial banking system dropped below $3.0 trillion in the past two years.

As can be seen from the chart, the total of reserve balances with Federal Reserve Banks tended to be well above three trillion for most of this time period.

As I have discussed many, many times over the past two years or so, the Federal Reserve had responded to the financial crisis in the 2020-2022 period by buying an excessively large amount of securities for its portfolio so as to "err on the side of monetary ease" during this period of time in order to avoid a greater financial disaster than what occurred.

The commercial banking system had lots and lots of "excess reserves" in its "vaults" and so was prepared for shocks to the system that might generate even more trouble for the banking system.

But, to get the commercial banking system back to a "more normal" position, the Federal Reserve needs to see these "excess reserves" get back to a more normal level. And, getting back to a "more normal" level means that the Fed will further reduce the securities it carries in its securities portfolio.

However, what is a "more normal" level?

One trillion?

$500.0 billion?

Or less?

And what about the effort by the Trump administration to get the Fed's policy rate of interest down by, say, maybe 200 basis points?

The reserves are there for this to happen.

But will the Federal Reserve allow the reserves just to go into loans, go into the reduction of the interest rate on loans, or go into an exploding money stock?

Or, there is the massive amount of government debt outstanding. Will the bank liquidity go into buying up the government debt that will be flowing throughout the economy?

In addition, there could be a change in the management of the Federal Reserve, as Jerome Powell retires next year and other board governors are appointed who support the views of President Trump. The "cash" to flood the economy with money is already there—it just has to be stimulated.

Here is the picture of the M2 money stock growth over the past five years.

The money is there. It just has to be put to more intensive use.

To my mind, Chairman Jerome Powell and the Federal Reserve have done a good job up to this point.

What the Federal Reserve has accomplished over the past three or four years has been remarkable.

But will we be able to say the same thing two years from now? Three years from now? Four years from now?

The Fed seems to have moved into another round of its "quantitative" monetary policy.

The last three and one-half years of policy have been, in my mind, outstanding.

We have not gone into a whirlwind of inflation. We have not experienced a financial collapse. We have an economy that is growing, although we would like it to grow faster. We have an inflation rate a little higher than the Federal Reserve policymakers would like—but it is down and not spinning out of control.

Unemployment is not too far above its recent lows, at an acceptable level, and ready to move a little bit lower.

And the stock market has recently been hitting more and more new historical highs.

Can the Federal Reserve make this all look a little better?

Or will it all fall apart?

data.title: EWZS: High Turnover, Low Returns, And Structural Weakness
data.description:
My last article focused on the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ) that invests in mid- and large-caps in South America's largest country. Given the cyclicality and the quality of the underlying businesses, I wasn't convinced that it offers value at current valuations.

Now, we're going to dive deeper into its little brother, the iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (NASDAQ:EWZS), to find out if it can offer more value and higher expected returns for patient long-term investors. Hint: It doesn't look too good!

In my articles, I usually neglect what I perceive to be short-term noise and the unknowable, also known as macro predictions. We're going to focus exclusively on the underlying holdings, their valuations, quality, and growth rates to determine the expected returns from here.

iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF

EWZS offers exposure to small public companies in Brazil, as it seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of small-cap Brazilian equities.

The net expense ratio is 0.60%, which could be considered high but is in line with other ETFs that offer single-country exposure.

The turnover rate is 103%, which seems excessive. I'll never understand why ETFs need to swap their holdings like they're running a hedge fund. It only leads to unnecessary transaction fees that add up over time. It's the first red flag.

P/E Ratio

According to WorldPeRatio, Brazil is one of the cheapest markets globally on a P/E ratio basis right now.

Yahoo Finance claims EWZS' P/E ratio is 9.72, while the Wall Street Journal (WSJ) puts it at 14.35. I usually rely on the WSJ numbers, as they tend to reflect the earnings multiple on a TTM basis quite accurately.

A 14x P/E ratio could be considered reasonable in today's exuberant market environment, but the underlying businesses must be decent to justify the price, which we're going to find out later.

Dividend Yield

Small-cap companies aren't usually known for high dividend yields, as small businesses tend to reinvest their earnings to achieve higher growth.

In 2024, EWZS distributed $0.46, which translates to a 3.3% dividend yield. In the first half of 2025, however, the dividend declined by almost 47%, and the dividend yield on a TTM basis fell to 2.63%.

The distributions since inception have been choppy. That's usually a sign that the underlying companies are highly cyclical and therefore less favorable for long-term investment.

In 2011, the first full year after inception, the dividend was $0.57. In 2024, the dividend was $0.46, and in 2025, it's likely to be even lower. Over 14 years, EWZS has shown negative dividend growth, which is a strong sign that the underlying companies are not only cyclical but also show negative growth in USD terms over the cycles. Another major red flag.

Sector Allocation

Consumer cyclical tops the sector list with 17.71% of total assets invested. It's followed by financials, consumer defensive, real estate, and technology.

Overall, the ETF seems diversified when it comes to sectors, given that it only holds 83 stocks.

Top Holdings

The top 10 holdings represent 35.96% of total assets. StoneCo Ltd., the largest holding, with 6.45% invested.

It's followed by Lojas Renner, Sendas Distribuidora, and Hapvida Participacoes Investimento. Let's dive deeper into the largest positions.

Top Holdings: Valuations, Growth Rates, and Quality

The most important factors to determine future returns are the starting valuations paid, the future growth rates, and the reliability of the cash flows, or in other words, the quality or moat of the companies.

Ideally, we want to buy high-quality companies with low debt levels and high growth rates at bargain-level prices. That's why just buying low P/E ratios or low P/B ratios isn't enough; we need to focus on quality as well.

First, let's quickly calculate the average P/E ratio, as well as the past growth rates of the top holdings.

Company P/E Ratio Annual EPS Growth Rate (Past 5 Years):
StoneCo Ltd Negative Nil
Lojas Renner 12.09 0.54%
Sendas Distribuidora 14.45 Nil
Hapvida Participacoes Investimento Negative Nil
Inter & Co. Inc 20.89 Nil
PagSeguro Digital 8.30 Nil
Smartfit Escola DE Ginastica E Danca 30.31 Infinite
Multiplan Empreendimentos Imobiliarios 15.16 19.90%
ALLOS SA 18.32 27.10
Cosan SA Negative Nil

(Gurufocus.com)

Just going through the cash flow statements of the largest holdings has been revealing. Most of the companies don't fit my criteria of what I'd perceive to be quality, to put it mildly. Thirty percent of the holdings didn't show any earnings to put a multiple on.

Only 2 out of 10 companies showed decent earnings and cash flow growth over the last 5 years. 80% either had negative earnings, lower earnings, or negative earnings 5 years ago. Those are not the characteristics of a promising long-term investment.

Of the companies that did show earnings, the average P/E ratio is 17.07. Not cheap given the overall quality of the portfolio. I rate EWZS a "sell".

Performance Since Inception

Since inception in 2010, EWZS investors have seen total returns of -13.49%, or a CAGR of -0.96%. I firmly believe in the concept of buying when there's blood in the streets, but in the case of EWZS, I believe the challenges are structural and not cyclical.

The high turnover rate, high fees, and unconvincing selection of equities will likely continue to lead to disappointing returns going forward.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Over the last decade, EWZS lagged the VanEck Brazil Small-Cap ETF (BRF), as well as EWZ, which focuses on large and mid-caps.

Given the quality of the underlying companies, I don't think there's a high chance of EWZS outperforming in the future unless there are changes to its structure

Conclusion

Emerging markets and small caps suggest high growth potential, but EWZS doesn't convince me. The combination of high turnover, underwhelming quality across its top holdings, and negative dividend growth over the long term paints a clear picture.

Unless there's a fundamental shift in the underlying index construction-or some clear evidence of higher business quality-I don't see a compelling case for long-term investors. I rate it a "sell."

data.title: Aura Minerals: Positioned To Double Production
data.description:
Introduction

I have been covering Aura Minerals (NASDAQ:AUGO) since June 2024, after meeting with the company's management, which provided insight into their operating process, capacity expansion, and cost curve. I then wrote about the Cerro Blanco acquisition, which has been renamed Era Dourada, and now update the view with higher gold prices and further M&A that should allow production to double by 2029.

More ADTV and Coverage

In the last two years, Aura listed its shares in Brazil, moved from the OTC to NASDAQ, and delisted from the Toronto exchange, creating substantial demand from the Brazilian and Latam investor base that has resulted in a significant increase in analyst coverage to 8 from 2 and average daily trading activity (ADTV) to US$9m from under US$1m.

Gold Price Sensitivity

In conversations with analysts that cover Aura, most utilize an average gold price based on the last 5 years, which, given the sharp increase, means that forward price estimates fall from the current levels. The current consensus data backs into a US$3,000 price long term vs. over US$3,700 today.

Using the consensus production, I estimated revenue and EBITDA if the gold price remains flat at US$3,700 through 2028. The difference is substantial at over US$200m in addition to EBITDA, which highlights the sensitivity the Aura may have to rising gold prices.

In addition, I added a second chart that highlights the production gap of a more aggressive view, given Aura's recent acquisition and intentions. The consensus has a 400k oz production level by 2028 vs. over 500k oz potential as outlined by the company but not yet committed to.

The combination of higher realized prices and production may make Aura far more compelling than current consensus estimates.

Consensus vs. Full Gold Price Estimates

Following the gold price sensitivity analysis above, I created a full set of financials with the current gold price and compared it to the consensus. The approximate $700 increase in price per oz adds US$100m to cash earnings (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) in 2026 and over US$200m in the 2027-2028 period. This also increases free cash flow (FCF) and reduces debt faster, as well as may allow for higher dividends. I did not use the higher production numbers, which would add another US$100m in FCF.

Valuation

The current YE26 price target is US$36.5, which does not provide much upside and backs into a 6.3x P/CE (cash earnings). Using the same multiple but at the US$3,700 gold price as analyzed earlier, I arrive at a US$43.8 price target of 25% upside. This expands to US$56 for 2027 on higher FCF. At the same time, Aura and many small-cap gold miners have a substantial valuation discount vs. large-cap gold miners that may shrink as Aura hits its growth and profitability targets.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data for a large- and small-cap gold mining company to gauge growth and valuation metrics. As can be seen, the large caps have a significant valuation premium despite generally lower growth rates. This may be due to ETF exposure, long production history, and reserve life. However, the valuation gap seems excessive, especially with the current gold price providing most gold miners with huge margins and profitability. In my view, the valuation gaps should shrink as markets search for new gold mining ideas. At the same time, these discounted valuations may attract M&A given the search for reserves and production.

Large caps have an average PE of 22x vs. 10x for small caps, while on a PEG basis, the large caps are at 4x vs. 0.3x for small-cap gold miners. Newmont (NEM), Agnico Eagle (AEM), Franco-Nevada (FNV), and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) seem very expensive from an absolute multiple and relative growth basis.

Risk

Without a doubt, the main risk is the price of gold, which will have a direct impact on earnings; the company does not hedge. There is also a risk to operations, accidents, increased environmental regulations, and even local citizenry protests that can cause closure or disruption.

Conclusion

I rate Aura a Buy. The company is set to double gold production and more than triple FCF at US$3,000/oz gold. At the same time, valuations are discounted vs. large caps, and the company pays an over 12% dividend yield on cost.

data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals: Strong Growth Trajectory And Commercial Expansion
data.description:
Introduction

Rigel Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RIGL) is a commercial-stage biotechnology company that is developing oral small-molecule kinase inhibitors, which are used to help treat blood disorders and certain cancers. Despite having three commercialized products and margins significantly above peers, the market continues to value Rigel as if it were a single-asset, high-risk biotechnology company.

Before we can fully understand the reasoning behind the investment thesis, we must first analyze how Rigel's small-molecule kinase inhibitors are helping individuals across the globe. These drugs help to turn off kinases, which are proteins that act like switches that tell a cell when to grow or divide. In many diseases, these switches can misfire and get stuck in the "on" position. This causes certain types of cancer and autoimmune disorders to attack the body at uncontrollable rates.

Currently, the company has three drugs in the process of commercialization, with another two in their potential pipeline. Firstly, there is TAVALISSE, which is helping to treat chronic immune thrombocytopenia in patients who haven't responded well to other treatment options. This disease causes platelets to be destroyed; without them, bleeding risk rises significantly, as platelets are essential for blood clotting. REZLIDHIA is their second drug, which is being used to treat acute myeloid leukemia. This type of cancer is usually resistant to chemotherapy, and those who have failed initial treatment often have very poor survival rates. GAVRETO helps to treat a subset of lung and thyroid cancers caused by genetic alterations in the RET gene, which leads to them being extremely resistant to chemotherapy and immunotherapy. Currently, markets are still treating Rigel as a one-drug biotechnology company, but its rapid ramp-up of GAVRETO and steady growth in REZLIDHIA are diversifying revenue faster than expected.

While many biotechs struggle with profitability for years, Rigel is already generating free cash flow with gross margins above 90%. The market is still valuing Rigel like a single-product risky biotech, but our analysis shows it trades at just ~2.8x forward sales, a significant discount to the peer median of 3.73x. This mispricing, despite improving diversification and positive free cash flows, forms the basis of our investment thesis.

Financial Snapshot

For Q2 FY2025, the company achieved total revenue of $101.7 million, which was made up of $58.9 million in net product sales (+76% YoY) and $42.7 million in contract/collaboration revenue. After we take their expenses into account, their net income comes out to $59.6 million, representing a net margin of 58.63%. For the first half of FY2025, the company had total revenue of $155.0 million, which included $102.5 million in product sales and $52.5 million in contract revenue. For 1H FY2025, their product sales grew 72% YoY. As of 6/30/25, the company had cash & cash equivalents of $108.4 million, which shows them having significant runway to fund their commercialization. For FY2025, they issued total revenue guidance of $270-$280 million, with net product sales at $210-$220 million and contract revenue at ~$60 million.

It is important to take into account that there was a one-time non-cash item of $40 million in this period. It's vital that we take this into account when considering their quality of revenue in our later valuation. It is also likely that the company will be capable of funding commercialization without near-term dilution due to their high margins and strong cash position.

Commercial Performance

TAVALISSE

The drug had Q2 FY2025 net product sales of $40.1 million, which represents +52% YoY growth. For 1H FY2025, they had net product sales of $68.5M, which represents ~44% YoY growth. Growth is being driven by ongoing U.S. growth, with management noting "consistent quarterly increases" in U.S. prescribing since mid-2023. Additionally, the company has achieved partnerships with multiple international pharmaceutical companies, which will help support royalty/partner revenue.

GAVRETO

The drug had Q2 FY2025 net product sales of $11.8 million, which represents a +521% YoY growth. Note that this high-growth percentage is due to the drug's initial launch. For 1H FY2025, they had net product sales of $20.8M. The company acquired U.S. rights in February of 2024, with an NDA transfer completed in June of 2024. Previously, the drug was under-marketed, but Rigel has created a dedicated oncology sales force, which should accelerate adoption going forward.

REZLIDHIA

The drug had Q2 FY2025 net product sales of $7.0 million, which represents +36% YoY growth. For 1H FY2025, they had net product sales of $13.1 million, representing a +31% YoY growth. It's important to note that this drug is licensed from Forma, which means that Rigel owes royalties that will suppress gross margins for this product. According to management's statements, this royalty sits at ~15% of net product sales.

Currently, TAVALISSE accounts for the majority of sales, contributing to about ~68% of total volume. This leaves GAVRETO accounting for ~20% and REZLIDHIA at ~12%. Over the past few years, their product portfolio has shifted towards a much more diversified base. In 2023, TAVALISSE accounted for over 80% of product sales. As GAVRETO has begun ramping up its commercialization, the share of TAVALISSE has fallen into the high 60s. This is extremely beneficial for the company as they are significantly de-risking their revenue base. This trend is also likely to accelerate as we predict that revenue growth of both GAVRETO and REZLIDHIA will outpace TAVALISSE as these other drugs continue through their adoption cycle. In turn, this should reduce reliance upon revenue from one source and provide much more stability in quarters to come.

Revenue Growth Model

Year TAVALISSE ($million) GAVRETO ($million) REZLIDHIA ($million) Contract Rev ($million) Total
2025E 140 45 30 60 275
2026E 155 (+11%) 58 (+28%) 35 (+17%) 20 268
2027E 170 (+10%) 75 (+29%) 41 (+17%) 20 306

Assumptions: Although TAVALISSE has shown growth above 40% for a few quarters, we assume that growth will moderate as the company begins reaching their total addressable market. For GAVRETO, we assume strong growth going forward due to their new sales team, which should contribute meaningfully to sales growth. For REZLIDHIA, the drug has demonstrated stable growth rates at ~30%; however, the model assumes this growth moderates due to the weaker margin performance in comparison to the other two products. We also assume that contract revenue stays consistent with the baseline from previous quarters.

Valuation Model

Year Revenue ($million) EBIT ($million) NOPAT ($million) FCF ($million) EBIT Margin
2025E 275 50 45 42 18%
2026E 268 57 51 49 21%
2027E 306 67 60 57 22%

Assumptions: For this valuation model, we consider the fact that the company has very high gross margins (95.57%). However, in any sale from REZLIDHIA, the company needs to pay 15% royalties. This leads us to the conclusion that blended COGS likely sits at ~10%. We also model SG&A as trending downwards because as the company scales, they will need less reinvestment in their oncology sales division. For this reason, we model it tapering down from 40% in 2025 to 35% in 2027. We also model their R&D spending in the low double digits as it has been stable at the level for many years. We apply a tax rate of 10% as they still have significant net operating losses from prior years, which will drag tax rates down until these are used up. We also set CapEx at 1% of revenue, as they outsource the majority of their production. Free cash flows will be discounted at 12%, in line with many other biotechnology companies of similar size and scale.

Pipeline Optionality

In addition to its commercial portfolio, Rigel has meaningful pipeline assets that the market appears to be ignoring. The most notable program is R289, a drug in early clinical testing for patients with myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS). MDS is a blood disorder where the bone marrow in the body doesn't make enough healthy blood cells, leading to problems such as excessive bleeding and infections. There are around 10,000 U.S. patients who are lower-risk and eligible for this drug. In a conservative case, we can assume that only 20% of those patients end up using R289. We then assume the company nets ~$140,000 per patient per year, which is in line with similar prescription drugs. This leads us to the estimate of around $300 million for peak U.S. sales.

However, we take into account the early stage that this product is in, which brings lots of uncertainty when considering it in valuation. For this reason, we apply a conservative 20% probability of success as well as assuming a 50% operating margin, which yields a risk-adjusted NPV of ~$100 million. Other than this, the company also has milestone and royalty rights under its collaboration with Eli Lilly. Assuming a 15% probability of success, these royalties are worth around $20 million on a risk-adjusted basis. This means that together, we have around $120 million in pipeline optionality to also consider in our valuation.

Forward EV/Sales Valuation

Using our FY2025 revenue projections of $275 million and an enterprise value of ~$769 million, Rigel currently trades at ~2.8x forward EV/Sales. In comparison to the sector median of 3.73x, the company is trading significantly below peers despite their exceptionally high margins and positive free cash flows. If Rigel were to simply trade in line with the peer median, we would get an implied share price of ~$63 per share. If we were to use FY2026 revenue of $268 million, Rigel currently trades at a ~2.9x forward EV/sales multiple. By FY2027, the revenues increase to $306 million, and our multiple falls to ~2.5x on today's enterprise value. This analysis shows the serious mispricing in today's valuation as its commercial mix is expanding and its operating performance is strengthening. It is clear that the company deserves a re-rating closer to peer levels, as it not only aligns with relative valuations but also with the company's strong fundamental positioning.

Key Risks

Despite Rigel's attractive growth opportunities and product diversification, there are still important risks we need to consider. The company is heavily reliant on TAVALISSE, meaning any slowdown in their product growth or increased competition could seriously impact revenues. In my opinion, the biggest risk stems from competition with GAVRETO, as it directly competes with Eli Lilly's Retevnmo. The long-term market penetration remains uncertain and will need to be evaluated on a quarterly basis to ensure growth in GAVRETO is on track with expectations.

Another big risk that needs to be considered for this investment is the pending success of R289. Our model assumes a 20% probability, but the real risk of failure is unknown. Lastly, any significant changes in market sentiment are likely to affect this company, as biotechs are highly sensitive to market movements.

Conclusion

Rigel Pharmaceuticals presents a fundamentally mispriced growth story. The company has now launched three commercial products that are contributing to revenue growth. In 1H FY2025, the company had total product sales grow 72% YoY. In addition to this, the company has issued total year guidance of $210-$220 million in product sales. GAVRETO is driving the majority of this growth with the addition of a full sales force, which had previously not been used. REZLIDHIA continues growing modestly even as royalty charges drag on margins, while TAVALISSE continues to deliver steady contributions to their growth. At an enterprise value of ~$769 million, Rigel trades at just ~2.8x FY2025 forward EV/Sales. Considering the fact that the peer median is 3.73x, if Rigel were to be valued in line with peers, then the implied share value would be ~$63 (~+108.05% from current share price). While competitive pressures remain, Rigel's high margins and strong cash-generating ability give it an unusually strong foundation for prolonged growth compared to typical small-cap biotechnology companies. As their product mix continues to diversify and their other drugs continue to ramp up, the current discount to peers is unsustainable. Rigel offers a rare setup for a biotechnology company as the market closes this clear valuation gap.

data.title: Avis Budget: Used Car Upside Not Materializing (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Avis Budget Group (NASDAQ:CAR) have been an excellent performer over the past year, basically doubling in value. Given its highly leveraged balance sheet, CAR's fair value can move significantly with modest changes in used car prices. Hopes that tariffs lead to higher used car pricing have indeed been a core driver of the stock. I last covered shares of Avis in May, rating the stock a "hold," though with shares up 35%, investors should not only have held CAR but also bought more. Given some indications used car demand may be slowing, as witnessed in CarMax's (KMX) recent results, now is a good time to revisit Avis Budget.

Essentially when valuing Avis Budget, investors need to focus on two things: its core rental business and the value of its large fleet of cars. Ultimately, the bulk of the company's value actually likely sits in its fleet rather than rental operations, so I will focus on that first. If anything, my enthusiasm here has dampened since May.

Used Car Tailwinds May Be Smaller Than Once Thought

Earlier this year, I was bullish on the potential for used car price appreciation, clearly a shared view given CAR's performance. The thesis was fairly simple. Post-COVID, we saw that supply tightness and higher prices in the new car market led to a substantial rise in used car pricing. This is largely due to the fact that higher new car prices likely push some buyers into the used car market, given affordability challenges. Given there is functionally a fixed supply of used vehicles, this incremental demand can swing prices meaningfully.

However, this is simply not playing out, at least not yet. While used car prices are higher than last fall, they remain lower than in February. I see a few reasons for this. First, new car prices have actually not moved as much as once expected, given delays and favorable rates negotiated by importers like the EU and Japan. We may eventually see some tariff-related new car inflation, but this is likely to be more modest, likely below 5% in my view, which is not sufficient to dramatically alter the landscape for used car prices.

Additionally, the prior spike in prices occurred at a time when consumers were flush with excess savings after COVID. After years of above-target inflation and solid spending, excess savings are much lower. This is likely limiting pricing power, especially on large purchases like cars, leading to lower transaction levels, given affordability strains. We also have had a quiet hurricane season, whereas eventful hurricane seasons tend to create demand by destroying some cars. Given these factors limiting demand, I do not see much incremental upside in used car pricing from here, though I also do not expect much of a downturn.

Beyond these macro headwinds, Avis's execution on monetizing its rental car position has been disappointing. For perspective, the company has $20.5 billion of vehicles, against which it has $19.9 billion of nonrecourse debt. Essentially each 1% move in vehicle values is worth nearly $6 per share. Now, I would note that its ~$600 million net equity position is likely too low because it took a vehicle impairment when prices were lower a few months ago that is likely to prove unnecessary in my view.

Still despite the solid used car pricing environment, Avis actually has $280 million in losses on vehicle sales so far this year. The problem is that it is taking longer to receive new cars from manufacturers, reducing its ability to sell older cars and harvest the gain. Additionally, 4% of vehicles are on recall, meaning it cannot sell them, and these are vehicles where it has relatively large gains. These headwinds are particularly challenging given they reduced the ability to sell during the seasonally strong summer season. Ultimately, I see some downside risk to my ~$3.5 billion in value from its fleet, given limited momentum in used car prices and further sale delays.

Rental Demand is Mixed With Limited Cash Generation

Its core business is benefiting from cost savings as it transformed its fleet over the past year, but demand is still fairly muted. In the company's second quarter, reported on July 29th, Avis Budget earned $0.10 as revenue ticked down slightly to $3 billion. While revenue was lower, adjusted EBITDA grew by 29% to $277 million given lower fleet costs.

In the Americas, revenue fell by 1% to $2.3 billion as revenue per day fell by 2% to $70.06. Vehicle utilization was up 50 bps to 70.7%. There has been an increasingly competitive pricing environment, which has weighed on rates. However, EBITDA expanded 18% to $220 million as per-unit fleet costs fell by 14% to $312 per month.

International revenue was also down 1% to $682 million, even as revenue per day rose by 3% to $58.5. Its average fleet shrank 4% to 181k units as it pulled back on fleet size given modest demand. Adjusted EBITDA grew by 71% to $82 given lower fleet costs.

So far in 2025, Avis has generated -$475 million of adjusted free cash flow as it put $500 million into its vehicle programs, which it expects to reverse out before year-end. Still, underlying free cash flow is just $25 million. While cost cuts are improving results, ongoing pricing power continues to greatly limit cash generation. I struggle to see leisure spending accelerate in a material way, which is likely to continue to pressure pricing power.

I would also note that aside from nonrecourse vehicle debt, Avis has $6.1 billion of corporate debt and $541 million of cash on hand. Importantly, it has no maturities until 2027, providing financial flexibility. Still, management is targeting $900 million to $1 billion of EBITDA this year, down from $1 billion previously. This leaves the company with well over 5x debt/EBITDA leverage, a very high level.

While Too Risky to Short, Investors Should Sell CAR Holdings

Now, as of this writing, CAR has a ~24% short interest, which is very elevated. As we have seen in the past, stocks with a large short interest can be susceptible to squeezes, and that is something investors must be cautious of in a market that has seen a renewed "meme" focus. That said, in the long term, as we have seen with GameStop (GME) and AMC (AMC), value does win out. Still, this technical factor would make me extremely cautious to short shares, especially in the current market environment.

Back in June, I assumed about $3.5 billion in used vehicle residual value and ~$800 million of EBITDA in its core business. We are seeing guidance move towards my $800 million level, and given the pressure on leisure spending, I continue to see downside risk to guidance, and I see EBITDA in the $800-$900 million range.

Even valuing this at 8x EV/EBITDA, that provides less than $25 of value after debt for shareholders. With $3-$3.5 billion of potential value in its used cars, I see $100-$120 as the overall fair value. With shares substantially above this, I now view CAR as a "sell." There is too much used car optimism in the price, and investors in CAR should sell shares. While technicals around its elevated short interest may support shares currently, value will win out over time in my view, and I would exit CAR.

data.title: Air France-KLM Is The Textbook Value Trap; Always Read Between The Lines
data.description:
When it comes to European air travel, Air France and KLM are almost omnipresent, from small regional airports to large international routes. But so was Pan Am in its heyday. As smart investors, you should always stay on your toes, which is why the earnings bump reported by Air France-KLM (OTCPK:AFRAF) (OTCPK:AFLYY) piqued my interest after its long-term downturn in share price. In this article, we investigate the recent earnings and what the underlying financial models say about the future of the stock.

For all intents and purposes, earnings and P/E are excellent measures of the value of a stock. It can really let you gauge the value that the market gives to each stock, whether it is undervalued or cheap for a reason; that is up to your own discretion. However, this stock is an example of why valuation ratios can be a major red herring when investing.

Last article, I looked deep into the earnings and free cash flows of IAG (OTCPK:BABWF) (OTCPK:ICAGY) and concluded that, despite its apparent excellent earnings, there was more to it than meets the eye. I ended up downgrading my "Strong Buy" to a "Buy" because of the underlying cash flows exposing investors to greater geopolitical risk than I was comfortable with. But as a comparison, let's look at another staple European airline brand and dissect Air France-KLM's H1 2025 earnings and market shares.

H1 2025

As is the trend with airlines this half, major gains in operating margins and earnings have been reported by KLM for H1, which is good; however, it stems from the same fuel price action inflating the earnings artificially. Moreover, the revenue increase this half, YoY, is just 5.6% compared to the revenue growth of its main competitors, with IAG growing 8% YoY and Lufthansa growing 6%.

This is compounded by the fuel costs, creating a €280,000,000 cushion in the operating margins, which themselves only rose by €384,000,000. Meaning, without the fuel price action over the last year, we would see less than a third of the operating profit increase we saw in this report and an H1 operating margin of only 1.58%. Far below the competitors of IAG but still exceeding Lufthansa's adjusted operating margin of -0.81%, excluding fuel price action.

Furthermore, revenue per available tonne kilometre (which is the total revenue divided by the total capacity of the airline multiplied by the distance travelled; a measure of the market demand for the airline, or how much capacity is wasted) is not incredible for Air France-KLM either. We can calculate the RATK using the figures below, taken from the H1 report.

Revenue (€MM): 12979

Available Seat Kilometers (ASKs) (MM): 137421

PLF: 86.7%

Assumed average passenger weight: 90kg

CTKs (MM): 7077

12979 / ((137421 * 0.867 * 0.09) + 7077) = €0.73 = $0.86 RATK

Compared to our average RATK for global airlines of $1.265 and competitors' RATKs of $1.21 (OTCPK:ICAGY) and $1.00 (OTCQX:DLAKY), this also leaves Air France-KLM trailing behind European competitors.

The Numbers

To cement our analysis of Air France-KLM in math, we will first look at their financial ratios compared to the wider market, taken from Seeking Alpha.

Metric GAAP Non-GAAP Industry GAAP Industry non-GAAP
PE 2.69 4.8 23.11 20.77
PEG 0.11 0.11 1.17 1.84
EV/EBIDTA 3.86 2.77 14.13 12.4

This implies that the shares are trading at a major discount for their earnings growth and current earnings; however, as we saw earlier, most of that can be attributed to fuel savings, not underlying financial success. We can figure out the true FCF potential of Air France-KLM by performing a DCF analysis.

We should then calculate the cost of equity (which is the performance over a year that the stock has to match to justify the risk of owning the stock compared to federal bonds)

Beta is taken at 1.90 from Yahoo, and market YoY growth is taken from the annualised return of the S&P 500 between 2014 and 2024 adjusted for inflation. Let's calculate the cost needed to justify owning this stock:

ERP = Predicted market YoY growth - Current 5Y federal bond yield = 8.3% - 4% = 4.3%.

Cost of Equity = Current federal bond yield + (Beta x ERP) = 4% + (1.90 x 4.3%) = 12.17%

We can use this as our discount rate to perform a basic 5-year 2-stage DCF model with Seeking Alpha Estimates, varying the model and terminal growth to obtain a range of upsides from the current share price.

Revenue: Projection of revenue using Seeking Alpha and other analyst estimates, working out at a 5-year CAGR of 15.51%.

EBIT: Earnings Before Tax projection using current EBIT * Revenue Growth Rate for the given year using Seeking Alpha estimates.

Tax: Taken from the mean average effective tax rates over the last 5 years (a 5-year CAGR of 17.6%), then applying this tax rate to the revenue for the given year from Seeking Alpha estimates.

NOPAT: Net Operating Profit After Tax is calculated using EBIT - Tax.

Depreciation: Taken from a 5-year average using Seeking Alpha estimates. (5YR CAGR of 4.529%).

Capex: Capital expenditures taken from analyst predictions, aligned with Seeking Alpha revenue CAGR post 2028.

Change in WC: Change in Working Capital is taken from the Current Working Capital multiplied by the Average Change in Working Capital over the last 5 years, then applying the same average change YoY at a CAGR of 20.32%.

FCFF: Discounted Free Cash Flow to Firm = (NOPAT + Depreciation - Capex - Change in WC) / ((1 + CoE) ^ (Years since 2024)).

FCFF w/out Capex: Also calculated and used as an example of the business's underlying cash flow prior to long-term investments.

During stage 1 of the model, called the growth stage, discounted FCFF is calculated, alongside discounted FCFF w/out Capex, for each year.

Note: values have been rounded up to the nearest million for display purposes only; calculations have been performed pre-rounding.

STAGE 1 2024 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029
Revenue (€MM) 31459 33247 34624 36449 35900 36340
EBIT (€MM) 1601 1976 2263 2489 2875 3321
Tax (€MM) 282 348 398 438 506 584
NOPAT (€MM) 1319 1628 1865 2051 2369 2737
Deprec (€MM) 2738 3273.3 3413 3558 3710 3868
CAPEX (€MM) 3728 3457 3550 4070 4701 5430
WC (€MM) 84 101 122 147 177 213
FCFF (€MM) 245 1198 1276 986 759 542
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€MM) 3973 4279 4098 3870 3728 3600

We then perform stage 2. This is called the terminal stage and takes the final FCFF and applies a terminal growth rate to the discount to find the value of the firm after the scope of the simulation. We will vary the terminal growth between -3% and 3% (which are my standard worst-best-case scenarios) to provide us with a reasonable spread of outcomes to use as a dataset in our models.

STAGE 2 -3% 0% 3%
FCFF (€MM) 2011 2507 3327
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€MM) 13362 16656 22105

Finally, we can obtain our valuation. The final intrinsic value comes from the sum of the discounted FCFFs and the terminal growth FCFF, divided by the number of outstanding shares (262.7 million).

Terminal Growth -3% 0% 3%
FCFF (€) 2.47 2.65 2.97
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€) 12.68 13.94 16.01

We can map each of the upsides or downsides from the current share price (€11.92) on a graph as a coefficient (from 1 for clarity of positives vs negatives) of the current share price.

Next, we can find how likely each potential discount is to push the share price above the cost of equity, justifying a buy. To do this, we will use a Monte Carlo simulation.

We will fluctuate each input coefficient randomly (log normal) from the above graph by +/- σ (the standard deviation) and plot each prediction's chance of exceeding the cost of equity by mapping it against a log normal curve formed of all the predictions. We are using the "log normal" curve because securities and stocks are often fat-tailed statistically. The graph plotted also contains a "target price," which is ascertained by multiplying the coefficient picked by the current share price.

A trendline is then formed to show the relationship between the P(x>CoE) and the target share price.

We have an R^2 value of 0.9823, which is extremely strong for the cubic trendline, meaning our representation of the relationship between P(x>CoE) and share price (with the distribution density resembling a fat-tailed log-normal curve) has a rock-solid relationship we can derive a price target from.

To find a reasonable rating, we will take the mean from the P(x>CoE) axis and then use the trendline to find the target price at said value. As there is an asymptote at 1, this should mitigate the target price being skewed by overly optimistic predictions derived from earnings growth. This is more important when our trendline is less strong; however, this method produces more reliable results.

Our average P(x>CoE) is 0.39, which is a 39% chance of exceeding the cost of equity. We can use the trendline to calculate our share price. An explanation of this seemingly low chance of exceeding the cost of equity is that the large spread between the high earnings-based results and the lower FCF-based results causes the log-normal distribution to favour the more frequent, but less extreme, FCF-based results.

-39.8095 * 0.39^3 + 149.68 * 0.39^2 - 49.103 * 0.39 + 5.4085 = €6.735

To obtain our rating, we can adapt and plot this against an empirical ratings curve:

Rating y = |(μ+4σ) - P(x)*100|/σ
Strong Sell >7
Sell 5 < y < 7
Hold 3 < y < 5
Buy 1 < y < 3
Strong Buy <1

|(μ+4σ) - P(x)*100|/σ = 4.88, which is between 3 and 5, which justifies giving Air France-KLM a "Hold" rating, with a price target of €6.735, which translates to $7.91 (OTCPK:AFRAF) and $0.791 (OTCPK:AFLYY).

In conclusion, never take value ratios as gospel when they are almost always based on short-term earnings, not the foresight of free cash flows. The analysis we performed on long-term FCF outlook and short-term valuation ratios also seems to output a rating and price target that reflects Air France-KLM's position against its local competitors. This reinforces my position that they deserve their "Hold" rating until revenue growth stabilises and improves, allowing for better debt management and FCF improvements.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Salesforce Is Now A Smart Buy (Upgrade)
data.description:
Early this year, I was bearish on Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) stock, but by June I turned neutral. I upgraded to a hold rating due to robust agentic AI demand but just a fair valuation. It seems like my cautiousness has paid off, as the stock is down around 6% since the publishing of that article. Today we'll be taking a deep dive into their most recent earnings while also discussing the long-term direction of the business.

In the below analysis, it is shown that the company's AI efforts are gaining traction. However, while Q2 financial results were quite promising, guidance is overall less than desirable. Currently, the valuation of Salesforce is rather depressed, and I believe this presents an opportunity even if AI monetization challenges persist for the near term. The company's leadership position in CRM cloud services positions them well for the agentic AI era, and so I have decided to upgrade the stock to a buy.

Agentic AI

We'll start off with a discussion on their AI push, as it will be key for long-term growth. Firstly, the company is continuing their push into agentic AI. Major companies like Dell Technologies (DELL) and FedEx (FDX) are now their Agentic Enterprise customers. With this emerging field of AI being highly important, strong adoption is a key signal of strength in my view. Salesforce reported that they have closed 12,500 deals since the launch of Agentforce, and on help.salesforce.com, Agentforce has processed more than 1.4 million requests. Therefore, it seems their innovation is providing real value.

In financial terms, their Data Cloud and AI business has hit $1.2 billion in annual recurring revenue. That is a 120% YoY increase. This growth rate is nearly unchanged from the previous quarter, and so demand is remaining very robust at this point. Lastly, it should be noted that "over 40% of Data Cloud and Agentforce Q2 bookings came from existing customer expansion." This shows that users have found Salesforce's solutions to be of value and is proof that AI spending is actually improving businesses significantly. To sum up their AI efforts, I would have to say that Salesforce seems well positioned, as all signs show that this emerging technology will be the key growth driver for the company in the years to come.

An Inflection Point

Salesforce reported their most recent financial results on September 3rd. For FY2026 Q2, the company generated total revenues worth $10.24 billion, up 9.77%. Firstly, while a sub-10% top-line growth rate isn't the most exciting, I believe investors have good reason to be encouraged by what they see. In their Q1, there was a very slight acceleration in growth that was barely noticeable. In Q2, there was clear acceleration from Q1's 7.62% increase in revenues. Therefore, it seems that an inflection point for growth is now in, and after many quarters of slowdown, there is finally a meaningful uptick. This could be a sign that AI strength is starting to power demand and is putting the company's trajectory in a better position. Salesforce also beat expectations by a reasonable margin of $98.41 million. For their subscription & support revenues specifically, they saw an 11% increase to $9.7 billion.

Remaining Performance Obligations

Despite the rebound in top-line growth, mixed RPO metrics show that demand may be slightly shaky. In the above table, the growth rates are shown for RPO. As you can see, total RPO growth dropped off a bit from Q1 and from the prior year period, and so while demand is resiliently strong, it isn't extraordinarily strong. With their AI business gaining traction, I would have perhaps expected some acceleration instead. In terms of cRPO in constant currency, the growth rate has been in the 10% to 11% range for the past six quarters, and so once again demand is solid but not accelerating at this point. Therefore, there may still be some AI monetization fears for the company right now.

Margin and EPS

In addition to the push into agentic AI, the company also seems to be making some improvements to their profitability. In adjusted terms, operating margin expanded by 60 basis points YoY to 34.3%. In the breakdown of non-GAAP expenses above, you can see that general & administrative expenses as a percentage of revenue dropped from 7% to 6% YoY, while research and development expenses increased to 12% from 11%. Therefore, in addition to improvement in overall business efficiency, the company is also shifting the use of cash to the right areas. R&D is basically investing in the future of the business, and so spending in this category has potential for return down the road. There weren't major changes to their expense profile, but nonetheless, the slight differences were for the better.

The top-line strength and efficiency improvements made it down to the bottom line. For Q2, Salesforce reported an adjusted EPS of $2.91, representing growth of 13.67% YoY. While that's also not a particularly high growth rate, the acceleration from the previous quarter's 5.74% increase should be noted. In addition, Salesforce also beat bottom-line expectations as they surpassed estimates by $0.13. Moving forward, you can see that analysts expect Q3 to be an even stronger quarter, but after that some moderation is possible. This is another sign that perhaps AI monetization remains as a concern. Let's discuss their guidance next.

FY2026 and Q3 Outlook

Salesforce revised GAAP operating margin and GAAP diluted EPS guidance downwards for the full year, but for key metrics, guidance was actually raised. For revenue growth, they raised their range from 8-9% to 8.5-9%. Back in FY2025, revenues grew by 9%, and so this guidance indicates there will be no major change in the growth rate. The lower end of the guidance being raised is definitely good news, but overall this new range shows that AI isn't expected to drive major growth yet in the annual time frame. For adjusted EPS, Salesforce raised their range from $11.27-$11.33 to $11.33-$11.37. At the midpoint of this revised range, they are implying YoY growth of 11.27%. This would be a deterioration from 24.09% of growth back in FY2025. Overall, I would have to say that full-year guidance still doesn't look too great and indicates that some monetization issues will linger for the near term.

For Q3, Salesforce is expecting revenue growth in the range of 8% to 9%. That shows there may be a slight deceleration again after the large Q2 acceleration in growth, and of course that is a disappointment. For adjusted EPS, they are giving a range of $2.84 to $2.86. This implies midpoint growth of 18.26%, which is very close to what analysts are now expecting. Therefore, while top-line guidance is undesirable, at least profitability will be strong. Lastly, let's talk about cRPO growth. Recall from earlier that constant currency cRPO growth was in the 10-11% range for multiple quarters. In Q3, the company expects constant currency cRPO growth to be just 9%. This is an indication that the trajectory of demand is levelling off. This could be a drag on future revenues, and so it is something investors should definitely pay attention to.

Valuation is Cheap

Despite the near-term challenges the company is facing, there are good reasons for picking up the stock now. Firstly, the forward P/E ratio is at one of the lowest levels of the past few years. The multiple was significantly above 30 to start 2025 and now finds itself at just 21.68. Note that the IT sector has a median non-GAAP forward P/E of 25.72 as of this writing, and so Salesforce seems to be trading at a significant discount. Yes, there are some AI monetization issues now, but the long-term looks quite bright. Taking a step back, Salesforce has been a leader in cloud computing and CRM for an extended period of time. With the company now integrating innovative agentic AI tools into their offerings, it will likely be a matter of time before their impressive customer base adopts and spends on their AI solutions. However, in the near term, top-line growth slowdown is expected, and so even though the valuation is low, a good amount of patience will likely be required before stock outperformance is seen.

Takeaway

Salesforce doesn't come without its flaws, but now is an opportunity to buy a potential AI leader for a cheap price tag. At the start of the article, it was discussed that Salesforce's AI efforts were gaining significant traction. Their Agentforce saw strong adoption, and ARR growth was impressive. While Q2 results exhibited a good direction for the business, guidance was rather uninspiring, suggesting that there are near-term AI monetization challenges. However, as stated above, given the company's dominant position in CRM and its strong relationships with major enterprises around the world, the company still seems to be in a great position to be a major provider of agentic AI in the years to come. With the valuation being at a discount, I believe now is a prime opportunity to buy in.

data.title: FTC Solar Has Almost Never Generated Profits And Is Very Leveraged
data.description:
FTC Solar (NASDAQ:FTCI) is a developer and installer of solar-tracking systems for large solar installations. The company's products help solar power plants increase the amount of energy collected from the sun.

The company has been very challenged in this market. It has almost consistently lost sales since it IPO'd in 2021 and presents severe losses, with operating margins close to negative 100%.

In addition, the company included going-concern commentary in its latest 10-K and has recently added significant debt. The debt temporarily reduces going concern risk but adds the weight of interest on profitability.

In this context, I believe the company is simply too risky for investment. It has not demonstrated that it can generate even gross profits, not even considering operating or net profits. It is shedding cash and can only finance it via debt and dilution. I initiate with a Hold rating.

Solar Trackers

FTC's products are tracker mountings for large solar farms. Solar panels are installed over these mountings, which then rotate them to absorb the highest amount of sun possible as the Earth rotates.

According to the company's presentation, approximately 90% of US large solar farms (>1 MW) use tracker technology. That is, it is a standard feature of solar installations. The company concentrates on the US market (90% of FY24 revenues).

Until very recently (2023), FTC concentrated on a slice of the tracker market (about 15% of the market according to the presentation) called 2P, or 2-panel trackers (the mounting installation holds rows of two panels in one set of pillars). Since this configuration is not in the highest demand, the company introduced the 1P configuration in 2023, significantly expanding the addressable market. 1P configuration trackers now make up 30% of the company's revenues (again, from the presentation).

Not the Best Economics or Results

Judging from FTC's financial history, the company cannot make the best economics out of this market. This is evidenced and can be explained by several factors.

First, overhead costs are significant. The company requires significant R&D (it has around 60 patents in the US) and specialized design (each project requires design work). It also requires manufacturing capacity (it invested in a small steel processor in 2023) and a sales force that carries long-term design-win-build work with clients.

These overhead costs generate two consequences.

First, without significant scale, the company is not profitable. The FTC's presentation claims that the company can reach adjusted EBITDA breakeven at $60 million, but the company's TTM revenues are $65 million, and it is still generating negative gross margins of more than 20% of revenues (2Q25 results).

Second, because the company has so much in terms of fixed, sunk costs, it has an incentive to add volumes at almost any price in order to dilute some of those costs. This leads to lower bargaining power with customers (higher willingness to cut variable margin in order to land a contract).

Two indications of low bargaining power are high customer concentration (72% of revenues from its 4 largest customers in 2024) and positive financing to customers via receivables. The company's receivables are almost as high as one year of sales and are also concentrated (74% of receivables from three customers). The company has to build and only later get paid for it, adding to cash strains.

Finally, and coming from context, the company's market is not in the best cyclical position since the company IPO'd in 2021. Solar has been on a downturn because of excess supply and low energy prices. This is probably behind the company's consistent revenue decreases since IPO in 2021 (somewhat arrested in FY24/25). A market in a cyclical downturn tends to have price competition and low margins.

Challenged Financial Position

With significant operational losses (operating margins are close to negative 100%), the company loses significant cash from operations.

Up to 2024, the company had financed those losses via equity. First, when it IPO'd in 2021 (at a valuation of $1.4 billion), and then via ATM offerings in 2023 and 2024. However, at its current CFO losses and market cap, the company would need to issue 1/3 of its equity every year only to stay afloat. Instead, it has recurred to debt.

In 2024, the company entered into $15 million in senior notes, with an original interest of 11% cash and 15% PIK, with hard-to-accomplish covenants ($100 million in revenues by 2025 and $200 million in 2026, plus $25 million in EBITDA by 2026). The FY24 10-K included going concern commentaries from management and the auditor.

In July 2025, after the end of the quarter (see Subsequent Events in 2Q25 10-Q), the company negotiated a $75 million credit line, which will be released in tranches (as of the 10-Q presentation, $13 million net had been received). The credit facility included renegotiations of interest on the Senior Notes (reducing to 5% interest in cash plus 7% PIK) and the removal of the mentioned covenants (under A&R Promissory Note in the 10-Q).

Still, the new facility also has strict conditions. Interest amounts to 5% cash plus 7% PIK. The company needs to maintain $20 million in unrestricted cash (meaning it cannot use as much as 1/3 of the loan but has to pay for it) and needs to reach undisclosed revenue and EBITDA targets in 2025/26.

In addition, the company has issued warrants for up to 6.8 million shares with a strike of $0.01 (meaning they are always on the money) and with cashless exercise. This implies the company has de facto diluted shareholders by 50% (basic shares outstanding of 13 million in 2Q25), because the warrants can be exercised at any time, and the resulting shares sold in the market.

With this financing, the company's financial risk has decreased significantly (although the 10-Q still includes going concern comments), but without operational improvements, the financing will only create some time before trouble appears again. In addition, considering that the whole facility is used, the company will have $90 million in debt, paying $4.5 million in yearly cash interest, and capitalizing interest at a rate of $6.3 million per year (compounding at 7%).

The Company is Too Risky for Investment

FTC has only rarely posted positive gross margins. This should already be a concerning factor for a company that is relatively mature (started operations in 2017 and has been public for 4 years).

It is not clear how much the company should expand in order to reach gross margin breakeven, and even less so for operating breakeven. The company comments on the $60 million revenue mark for adjusted EBITDA breakeven, but so far in FY25, it has generated adjusted EBITDA losses of $10 million per quarter, despite building revenues at an annualized rate of $70 million in 1H25.

In addition to this, the solar market does not seem to be in the best position currently, with significant federal support removed by a US administration that has on several occasions expressed a preference for other forms of energy.

Finally, the company has obtained financing that can help it keep its operations going, but this financing comes at a significant cost, as explained above, and may not last for long. Using adjusted EBITDA loss as a proxy for net cash outflows from operations, the company is losing $40 million in cash per year (CFO comes in at $30 million TTM) at current run rates. With $75 million in new financing, of which $20 million has to be kept as unrestricted cash, the company has extended its run rate by less than 1.5 years ($55 million over $40 million is 1.375 years). In addition, the debt covenants (not fully disclosed yet) might be hard to reach in terms of revenue growth and breakeven profitability.

For these reasons, I believe the name is not attractive for investment. The company's equity is at severe risk of dilution in order to finance operations, and even if all debt were converted to equity, the company is very far from generating even the most basic levels of profitability. In order to qualify for a Buy rating, the name should be much closer to operational profitability and largely reduce its leverage (probably via dilution).

FTC Solar is a clear pass, and I initiate with a Hold rating.

Liquidity Risks

FTCI's average trading volume this year (after a reverse 1-for-10 split in November 2024) has been around 100 thousand shares per day. With a share price of about $8, this implies a liquidity of about $800 thousand per day, or close to 1% of the company's market cap ($112 million currently).

This is not super low liquidity in relative terms, but it is not high in absolute terms, which implies that the stock price can move violently up or down with or without news. This is a risk for both long and short positions on the name.

It is this lack of absolute liquidity that leads me to issue a Hold instead of a Sell. A negative position on a low-liquidity name that is already overvalued can quickly backfire with good news.
